does: TrialCalibre automates and scales the BenchExCal framework for causal effect estimation in real-world evidence studies. The system uses a multiagent architecture with specialized agents—including Orchestrator, Protocol Design, Data Synthesis, Clinical Validation, and Quantitative Calibration agents—to execute a two-stage process: first benchmarking an observational trial emulation against an existing RCT, then using observed divergences to calibrate effect estimates for new clinical indications. The framework incorporates reinforcement learning from human feedback and knowledge blackboards to enable adaptive calibration while maintaining transparency and auditability throughout the causal inference pipeline.
connects: This work builds on the BenchExCal framework, which addresses the persistent challenge of residual biases in real-world evidence studies that emulate target trials. TrialCalibre extends prior research in causal inference, trial emulation, and observational study design by automating workflows that typically require substantial manual coordination. It responds to the growing demand for credible RWE in regulatory and clinical decisions by systematizing the comparison of observational data against RCT benchmarks—a comparison-based approach rooted in decades of observational study methodology. The integration of multiagent systems and reinforcement learning from human feedback reflects broader trends in automating complex, multidisciplinary scientific workflows.
enables: TrialCalibre enables large-scale, rapid calibration of observational emulations across multiple clinical indications and therapeutic areas, reducing the resource burden that previously limited BenchExCal's deployment. By automating protocol design, data synthesis, and quantitative calibration, the system could facilitate faster evidence generation for regulatory submissions and clinical guideline development. The framework's emphasis on transparency and auditability via knowledge blackboards supports broader adoption of trial emulation in regulated settings. Future work could extend the architecture to handle more complex causal structures, integrate real-time observational data streams, or apply learned calibration patterns across related therapeutic domains to further accelerate real-world evidence synthesis.
